title: What is a Dense Representation?
concept: A dense representation is a compact learned vector where many dimensions work together to capture useful traits, unlike one-hot or hand-built sparse features that spread information across huge mostly-empty spaces.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What made residual networks such a big breakthrough in the last unit?

options.text: They made very deep models much easier to train in practice.
They removed the need for hidden representations entirely.
They turned every task into nearest-neighbor search.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Once deep models became easier to train, they could also learn richer internal representations instead of relying only on manual feature design.

cards.title: Too many suitcases, too little meaning
cards.body: Imagine describing one traveler by sending thousands of mostly empty suitcases, where only one or two contain anything useful. That is how one-hot and other sparse feature systems can feel: a huge format carrying very little signal in each example. Dense representations pack the meaning into a much smaller bag.

cards.title: Compact vectors that learn what matters
cards.body: A dense representation is a short learned vector in which many numbers can each carry part of the meaning. Instead of using a giant mostly-zero pattern like a one-hot vector, the model learns a compact code whose dimensions work together. This is useful because similar inputs can share structure instead of living in completely separate buckets.

cards.title: A folded map instead of a wall-sized poster
cards.body: A giant poster map may show every street, but it is awkward to carry around. A folded travel map keeps the important structure in a compact form you can actually use. Dense representations do something similar: they compress useful patterns into a smaller, workable space.

cards.title: Words do not need their own private switch
cards.body: Suppose a vocabulary has 50,000 words. A one-hot encoding gives each word its own separate position, so dog and puppy look completely unrelated. A dense learned vector can instead represent both with compact features that partly overlap, which helps the model reuse what it learns about similar words.

cards.title: Dense does not mean magically human-readable
cards.body: A common myth is that each coordinate in a dense vector must have an obvious plain-English meaning like “friendliness” or “size.” Sometimes a dimension lines up with a human idea, but often meaning is spread across several coordinates together. The power comes from the whole pattern, not from each number being easy to label.

cards.title: Sparse identity vs. shared compact meaning
cards.body: Why learned dense vectors often scale better than giant sparse codes:

left.text:
Huge mostly-zero vectors
Each item often isolated from others
Manual design or one-hot buckets dominate

right.text:
Short learned vectors
Many dimensions share the load
Similar items can share structure

exercises.prompt: Why can one-hot features be limiting for large vocabularies or item catalogs?

options.text: They create huge vectors where each item sits alone, so the model cannot easily share meaning across similar items.
They make every model automatically overfit on the first batch.
They force all coordinates to become negative.
They stop neural networks from using matrix multiplication.

exercises.explain: Correct. One-hot encodings are simple, but they do not naturally express that some items are more alike than others.

exercises.prompt: True or false: In a dense representation, useful meaning is usually spread across several coordinates working together.
exercises.explain: True. Dense vectors are powerful partly because they distribute information across the whole pattern.

exercises.sentence: A learned vector with many nonzero coordinates sharing the job is called a ___ representation.
exercises.explain: Exactly. “Dense” highlights that the vector packs information into a compact set of active dimensions.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best shows the advantage of a dense representation?

options.text: A model learns short vectors where related products share some structure instead of living in totally separate one-hot buckets.
A model gives every product a single giant ID column and assumes similar products need nothing in common.
A model refuses to learn any internal features and only stores raw labels.

exercises.explain: Right. Dense representations are valuable because they let related items share learned structure.

exercises.prompt: You are building a language model with tens of thousands of words. Which representation choice best supports compact storage and shared meaning between related words?

options.text: Learn dense vectors so similar words can share structure in a compact space.
Use only one-hot vectors so every word stays completely isolated from every other word.
Remove hidden layers because representations are unnecessary for language.
Assign random IDs and hope the model treats nearby integers as similar.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Dense learned vectors give the model a compact way to reuse structure across related words instead of treating every word as an island.
